news_summary: The UN encourages continued commitment to achieving Sustainable Development Goals despite ongoing challenges.
news_summary_fr: L'ONU encourage à maintenir l'engagement en faveur de la réalisation des Objectifs de développement durable malgré les difficultés persistantes.
news_summary_es: La ONU anima a mantener el compromiso con la consecución de los Objetivos de Desarrollo Sostenible a pesar de los retos actuales.

articles.title: Despite hurdles, don’t give up on Sustainable Development Goals, UN urges
articles.summary: In a world of “parallel realities” where stark inequalities seemingly divide people and challenge the promise of multilateralism, the vision of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) is to bring people together and help them achieve a better reality.
articles.content:
President of key UN body ECOSOC, Lok Bahadur Thapa, reminded the international community on Monday that no country can achieve sustainable development on its own, and that political will and momentum is necessary to achieve the SDGs by 2030.

UN Photo/Eskinder Debebe

“Let us be judged by results,” Mr. Thapa said.

The speech marked the beginning of the top ministerial level meeting of the High-Level Political Forum on Sustainable Development (HLPF), the UN’s annual forum to assess progress on the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) which is convened by ECOSOC – the UN Economic and Social Council.

The High-Level Segment will conclude on Thursday with a negotiated declaration between government ministers and heads of State on concrete actions Member States will take to advance progress on the 17 Goals.

The latest draft of the 2026 ministerial declaration includes commitments to increase investment in the SDGs and develop international rules for transformative technologies such as artificial intelligence.

Tweet URL

By the end of the HLPF, 36 countries will have presented so-called Voluntary National Reviews (VNRs) on their progress in achieving the SDGs and shared best practices with other Member States.

As the HLPF kicked off last Tuesday in New York, the UN simultaneously released its annual SDG Progress Report, finding that coordinated action on the goals has succeeded but governments must urgently accelerate action if they are to be met by their 2030 deadline.

Goals need financing

Opening Monday’s session, Mr. Thapa, UN Secretary-General António Guterres and General Assembly President Annalena Baerbock, admitted that the SDGs were not on track.

At the same time, they emphasized that the UN and its Member States know what works to create sustainable development, but that financing remains the biggest obstacle to quick and effective action.

In fact, the principal finding of the SDG Progress Report – that only 36 per cent of SDG indicators with trend data are on track or making moderate progress – reflects a lack of effective financing rather than a lack of goodwill, according to Mr. Guterres.

He noted that developing countries pay borrowing costs several times higher than rich countries, and that many spend more on debt service than national services, making them ill-equipped to implement the SDGs without assistance.

“We need to increase the voice and participation of developing countries, reforming the global financial architecture to ensure those countries receive the support they need,” Mr. Guterres said.

To increase SDG financing, Mr. Guterres stressed the need for reforms to the multilateral development banks to unlock private investment and ensure equitable access for developing countries.

UN Photo/Eskinder Debebe

Jaewon Choi, leader of the DMUN Foundation, a youth-led, non-governmental organisation that empowers young people to engage in multilateral processes, also weighed in, demanding that young people be included in initiatives to achieve the Goals.

“A revitalized UN must integrate meaningful youth engagement,” Mr. Choi told ministers.

Promise of multilateralism

The speakers urged Member States to prioritise SDG financing, debt relief, civil society partnerships and concrete collective action as they negotiate.

Mr. Guterres highlighted the Pact for the Future, Sevilla Commitment, Doha Political Declaration, High Seas Treaty and Antigua and Barbuda Agenda for Small Island Development as evidence that multilateralism can still deliver.

“Development requires all hands on deck – businesses, development banks, innovators, civil society, community leaders and young people,” Mr. Guterres said.

UN Photo/Eskinder Debebe

The very first words of Ms. Baerbock’s address to ECOSOC – “We are not on track” – set the stage for the UN’s call to Member States at the HLPF: despite immense challenges, giving up on achieving the SDGs by 2030 is not an option.

On target

As a noted football fan, Ms. Baerbock ended her speech with a timely comparison between the SDGs and this year’s gripping football World Cup competition.

“It has told us very clearly one thing: to never give up at minute 80,” Ms. Baerbock said. “One goal at the right moment can change everything. And 17 goals at the same moment can change the world. It is up to us to play the last four years together.”
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the ongoing global commitment to achieving the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) by 2030, emphasizing the importance of multilateral cooperation, increased financing, and inclusive participation. It reports on a significant international forum where 36 countries share progress and best practices, and where leaders call for concrete actions and reforms to overcome financial and political challenges. The story has broad significance for global development and sustainability efforts, providing hope and motivation to continue striving for these goals despite obstacles.
articles.personality_title: UN leaders call for stronger teamwork and funding to reach 2030 development goals
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) are a set of 17 global targets set by the United Nations to improve life around the world by 2030. These goals include ending poverty, improving health, and protecting the environment. Countries meet every year at the High-Level Political Forum (HLPF) to check progress and plan next steps.

**What happened** – At the HLPF meeting in New York, UN leaders and representatives from 36 countries shared updates on their progress toward the SDGs. They agreed that despite challenges, such as lack of enough money and political will, efforts must continue. The UN Secretary-General António Guterres and others highlighted the need for better financing, especially for poorer countries that struggle with high borrowing costs. They also emphasized including young people in planning and decision-making.

**Impact** – This meeting showed that many countries are working together and sharing ideas to reach the SDGs, but more funding and cooperation are needed to speed up progress. The focus on helping developing countries and reforming financial systems is important because it can make a big difference in achieving the goals. The involvement of youth groups signals a broader and more inclusive approach to solving global problems.

**What's next step** – The forum will end with a declaration where countries promise concrete actions to improve investment in the SDGs and create rules for new technologies like artificial intelligence. Leaders hope that reforms at international banks will unlock private investments and help poorer nations. The next few years are critical to keep pushing forward and to work together more closely.

**One-sentence takeaway** – UN leaders stress that reaching the 2030 Sustainable Development Goals requires stronger teamwork, more funding, and including everyone, especially young people, in the effort.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les dirigeants de l’ONU appellent à plus de coopération et de financement pour atteindre les objectifs de développement 2030
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les Objectifs de développement durable (ODD) sont 17 cibles mondiales fixées par l’ONU pour améliorer la vie dans le monde d’ici 2030. Ces objectifs incluent la lutte contre la pauvreté, l’amélioration de la santé et la protection de l’environnement. Chaque année, les pays se réunissent au Forum politique de haut niveau (FPHN) pour faire le point et planifier les actions.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Lors de la réunion du FPHN à New York, les dirigeants de l’ONU et des représentants de 36 pays ont partagé leurs progrès vers les ODD. Ils ont reconnu que, malgré des défis comme le manque de financement et de volonté politique, les efforts doivent continuer. Le Secrétaire général António Guterres et d’autres ont souligné la nécessité d’un meilleur financement, surtout pour les pays les plus pauvres qui ont des coûts d’emprunt élevés. Ils ont aussi insisté sur l’importance d’impliquer les jeunes dans la planification et la prise de décisions.

**Impact** – Cette réunion montre que de nombreux pays coopèrent et échangent des idées pour atteindre les ODD, mais qu’il faut plus de fonds et de collaboration pour accélérer les progrès. L’accent mis sur l’aide aux pays en développement et la réforme des systèmes financiers est important car cela peut grandement aider à atteindre les objectifs. La participation des jeunes montre une approche plus large et inclusive pour résoudre les problèmes mondiaux.

**Prochaine étape** – Le forum se terminera par une déclaration où les pays s’engagent à agir concrètement pour augmenter les investissements dans les ODD et établir des règles pour les nouvelles technologies comme l’intelligence artificielle. Les dirigeants espèrent que des réformes des banques internationales permettront de débloquer des investissements privés et d’aider les pays pauvres. Les prochaines années seront cruciales pour continuer à avancer ensemble.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Les dirigeants de l’ONU soulignent que pour atteindre les ODD de 2030, il faut plus de coopération, de financement et l’inclusion de tous, surtout des jeunes.
articles.personality_title_es: Líderes de la ONU piden más cooperación y financiamiento para lograr los objetivos de desarrollo 2030
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los Objetivos de Desarrollo Sostenible (ODS) son 17 metas globales establecidas por la ONU para mejorar la vida en todo el mundo para 2030. Estos objetivos incluyen acabar con la pobreza, mejorar la salud y proteger el medio ambiente. Cada año, los países se reúnen en el Foro Político de Alto Nivel (FPAN) para revisar el progreso y planificar los próximos pasos.

**Qué pasó** – En la reunión del FPAN en Nueva York, líderes de la ONU y representantes de 36 países compartieron sus avances hacia los ODS. Reconocieron que, a pesar de los desafíos como la falta de financiamiento y voluntad política, los esfuerzos deben continuar. El Secretario General António Guterres y otros destacaron la necesidad de un mejor financiamiento, especialmente para los países más pobres que enfrentan altos costos de endeudamiento. También subrayaron la importancia de incluir a los jóvenes en la planificación y toma de decisiones.

**Impacto** – Esta reunión mostró que muchos países están cooperando y compartiendo ideas para alcanzar los ODS, pero se necesita más dinero y colaboración para acelerar el progreso. El enfoque en apoyar a los países en desarrollo y reformar los sistemas financieros es importante porque puede marcar una gran diferencia. La participación de los jóvenes indica un enfoque más amplio e inclusivo para resolver problemas globales.

**Próximo paso** – El foro concluirá con una declaración donde los países se comprometen a tomar acciones concretas para aumentar la inversión en los ODS y crear reglas para nuevas tecnologías como la inteligencia artificial. Los líderes esperan que las reformas en los bancos internacionales desbloqueen inversiones privadas y ayuden a las naciones más pobres. Los próximos años serán clave para seguir avanzando juntos.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los líderes de la ONU insisten en que alcanzar los ODS para 2030 requiere más trabajo en equipo, financiamiento y la participación de todos, especialmente de los jóvenes.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant, detailed painting of a diverse group of interconnected hands forming a circle around 17 glowing orbs, each orb symbolizing a Sustainable Development Goal, set against a backdrop of a rising sun over a stylized globe with subtle outlines of continents, using warm, natural earth tones and soft blues to evoke hope, unity, and collective global effort.
